title: Genome: The Autobiography of a Species in 23 Chapters

authors.name: Matt Ridley
authors.role: author, science writer, former editor of The Economist

awards: Shortlisted for the Samuel Johnson Prize (2000)
themes: The human genome as a historical document of our species
Each chromosome tells a story about human nature
The interplay of genes and environment in shaping behavior
The evolutionary echoes carried in every genome
How genes reveal human prehistory and migration patterns
The promise and peril of genetic medicine
The political implications of genetic knowledge
Selfish genes, cooperation, and the origins of morality
The paradox of genetic individuality within shared humanity
excerpt: A chromosome-by-chromosome tour of the human genome that reveals how our DNA carries the history of our species — from a common ancestor four billion years ago to the genetic echoes of ancient invasions in our cells today.
authorBiography: Matt Ridley is a British journalist, businessman, and popular science author. Born in 1958 in Northumberland, England, he studied zoology at Oxford and earned a DPhil in 1983. He served as science editor and Washington correspondent for The Economist before becoming a full-time author. His books include The Red Queen: Sex and the Evolution of Human Nature (1993), The Origins of Virtue (1996), Genome (1999), Nature via Nurture (2003), The Rational Optimist (2010), The Evolution of Everything (2015), and How Innovation Works (2020). He was chairman of the UK government's Northern Rock bank between 2004 and 2007. Ridley was appointed a life peer in the House of Lords in 2013. His work has been translated into over 30 languages.
authorKnownFor: Genome: The Autobiography of a Species in 23 Chapters (1999)
The Red Queen: Sex and the Evolution of Human Nature (1993)
